skill.name: managing-commits
skill.description: Helps users write, review, group, and validate Git commits using conventional commit rules, commit quality checks, and optional GitHub issue references.
skill.summary: Improve Git commit quality with conventional commit guidance and issue-aware helpers.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found no malicious intent or prompt injection. Most command findings are expected git and gh subprocess calls for commit analysis and GitHub issue lookup. Risk remains medium because the skill can execute local repository commands, contact GitHub through the gh CLI, and cache repository and issue metadata locally.

medium_findings.title: Local Git and GitHub CLI Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts execute git and gh commands to inspect commits, changed files, repository metadata, projects, labels, and issues. Commands use argument arrays rather than shell strings, which lowers injection risk, but they still run local tools and can expose repository context to GitHub CLI operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Subprocess usage is directly present and semantically tied to git and GitHub workflows. The risk is moderate because arguments are structured lists and no shell=True execution was found.

medium_findings.title: GitHub Issue Metadata Cached Locally
medium_findings.description: The issue tracker fetches open GitHub issue fields including titles, labels, assignees, URLs, and a body preview, then writes them to a local cache. This is useful for issue-aware commits, but it may store sensitive private repository issue context on disk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes and cached fields are explicit in the reviewed code. No exfiltration endpoint beyond expected GitHub CLI calls was found, so this is a privacy and local storage concern rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography detections correspond to commit-message examples, JWT terminology, and commit hash placeholders. No evidence found that these files implement MD5, SHA1, or another weak cryptographic algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation and sample commit text, not executable cryptographic code. Multiple flagged lines mention hashes or JWTs only as commit content examples.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many static external-command hits in markdown are code fences or inline command examples for commit workflows. They document user-facing Git and tooling commands rather than executing code directly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged references are inside markdown examples and explanatory text. They may guide users to run commands, but the markdown itself is not an execution path.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: Reviewed skill instructions and referenced files did not contain text asking the evaluator to ignore instructions, override security review, or treat the skill as pre-approved.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found normal skill activation instructions and no prompt-injection phrasing. This is still limited to the reviewed files and static-analysis file set.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Calls to Repository Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: Python subprocess.run is used to call git and gh. This is expected for the skill but should be visible to users before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are present in executable Python files. Their use is legitimate for git automation, but it is still a sensitive execution pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Cache Writes Under .claude
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts write environment and issue cache files under .claude/github-workflows, which can include repository, branch, user, and issue metadata.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cache paths and writes are explicit. The data is local, but it can contain sensitive private project metadata.

content.value_statement: Poor commit messages make history hard to review and automate. This skill guides conventional commits, analyzes history, groups changed files, and suggests issue references.
actual_capabilities: Generates conventional commit messages with type, scope, body, and footer guidance.
Validates commit messages against conventional commit format and length rules.
Analyzes recent Git history for format, size, vague messages, and fixup candidates.
Groups changed files by scope and likely commit type for atomic commits.
Caches GitHub issues locally and suggests commit footers such as Closes or Refs.
Provides reference material for breaking changes, reverts, co-authors, and changelog workflows.
limitations: Requires a Git repository for history analysis and changed-file grouping.
GitHub issue integration requires the GitHub CLI and an authenticated account.
Generated commit messages still need human review for project-specific intent.
The helper scripts execute local git and gh commands, so users should review before running them.

use_cases.title: Write a Clean Feature Commit
use_cases.description: Create a conventional commit message that explains a focused feature change and links the related issue.

use_cases.title: Review Branch Commit History
use_cases.description: Check a branch for vague subjects, oversized commits, missing bodies, and non-conventional messages before review.

use_cases.title: Prepare Release Notes
use_cases.description: Use conventional commit types and issue references to make changelog generation more reliable.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Commit Message
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: create one message from a short change summary.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a conventional commit message for this change: [describe the change]. Include a short subject and optional body.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Existing Message
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: rewrite a weak message into a clear conventional commit.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this commit message and improve it using conventional commits: [paste message]. Explain the main changes.

prompt_templates.title: Group Staged Changes
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: split mixed changes into atomic commits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my staged changes and suggest logical commit groups with conventional commit subjects.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Branch History
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: assess a branch before merging.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the commits on branch [branch name] for format, size, clarity, fixup candidates, and missing issue references.

output_examples.input: A developer changed authentication token refresh behavior and wants a commit message.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: feat(auth): add token refresh flow. The body explains why sessions remain active and the footer can close the related issue.

output_examples.input: A branch contains several vague messages such as update code and fixed stuff.
output_examples.output: The review flags vague subjects, recommends specific conventional types, and suggests squashing temporary commits before merge.

output_examples.input: Staged files include API changes, tests, and documentation.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests separate feature, test, and documentation commits so each commit has one logical purpose.

best_practices: Use one logical change per commit and split unrelated files before committing.
Include issue references when the commit completes or relates to tracked work.
Review generated subjects for accuracy before running any commit command.
anti_patterns: Using vague subjects such as update, fix stuff, or changes.
Combining feature code, tests, formatting, and documentation in one large commit.
Running helper scripts without understanding the git or gh commands they execute.

faq.question: Does this skill create commits automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide commit creation and includes scripts that may run git commit when used interactively.

faq.question: Does it require GitHub?
faq.answer: Basic commit guidance does not require GitHub. Issue tracking features require the GitHub CLI.

faq.question: Can it validate existing commit history?
faq.answer: Yes. The analyzer can inspect recent commits or a branch and report quality issues.

faq.question: Can it help split large changes?
faq.answer: Yes. The file grouping helper suggests logical groups based on paths, scopes, and change types.

faq.question: Does it store data locally?
faq.answer: Yes. GitHub issue and environment context may be cached under .claude/github-workflows.

faq.question: Is the output compatible with changelog tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It follows conventional commit patterns commonly used by changelog automation.
